content:
Why We Haven’t Seen a New Friday the 13th Movie in 16 Years Sixteen years.
That’s how long horror fans have been waiting for a new Friday the 13th movie. In an era where horror franchises get rebooted more often than we change our phones, the absence of Jason Voorhees from theaters feels downright suspicious. Here’s what’s really going on with our favorite hockey-mask-wearing, machete-wielding killer.
The Birth of a Horror Icon The Friday the 13th story kicked off in 1980. Director Sean S. Cunningham, fresh off seeing John Carpenter’s Halloween print money, wanted his own slice of horror pie. The original film?
Made on a shoestring budget of just $550,000. That was cheap even back then. Then lightning struck. The movie raked in nearly $60 million worldwide. That’s a 10,000% return on investment!
Cunningham never planned to launch a franchise. This was supposed to be a one-and-done flick. But audiences couldn’t get enough of the terror at Camp Crystal Lake. The studio smelled money. They wanted more. A lot more.
What followed wasn’t just a sequel—it was a horror empire:
Twelve slasher films A television series Novels and comic books Video games Enough merchandise to fill a horror museum (and then some) Jason Voorhees: From Boy to Boogeyman Here’s a shocker: Jason Voorhees wasn’t even the killer in the first Friday the 13th movie. His mom, Pamela Voorhees
, did all the dirty work, seeking revenge for her son’s drowning at Camp Crystal Lake while the counselors were busy… well, not paying attention.
Jason didn’t become the main villain until the second film. And that iconic hockey mask? Didn’t show up until Friday the 13th Part III. Before that, Jason wore a burlap sack over his head—creepy for sure, but not exactly the look that would launch a thousand Halloween costumes.
The hockey mask, by the way, was just a modified Detroit Red Wings goalie mask. Now it’s one of the most recognizable symbols in horror history—right up there with Freddy Krueger’s glove and Leatherface’s chainsaw.
The Cultural Phenomenon The Friday the 13th franchise isn’t just movies—it’s embedded in our culture:
Inspired countless imitators and parodies (seriously, try counting them) Popped up in everything from The Simpsons to rap lyrics
Made “Friday the 13th” shorthand for bad luck in American culture Generated billions in merchandise revenue Launched careers (including one of Kevin Bacon’s earliest movie appearances) I’ve always loved how these movies, for all their gore and jumpscares, became this cultural shorthand. Even people who’ve never seen a slasher film know who Jason is. That’s staying power.
The Franchise-Killing Problem So why no Jason movie since 2009? The answer is frustratingly simple: rights issues.
Unlike most horror franchises that live comfortably with one studio, Friday the 13th is stuck in a legal nightmare:
The franchise bounced between Paramount and New Line Cinema for years When Warner Bros. bought New Line Cinema in 2008, everything got messier Writer Victor Miller reclaimed the rights to the original screenplay Now different parties own different pieces of the Jason puzzle In 2018, Miller won a lawsuit that returned the rights to the original film to him. But this wasn’t a total victory—other elements stayed with different stakeholders. A legal stalemate.
For a hot minute there, it looked like a new movie might happen. As part of a deal for Warner Bros. to get international rights to Interstellar, Paramount briefly secured full theatrical rights. But Paramount was having financial problems in the mid-2010s, and despite setting release dates for 2016 and 2017, the studio never started filming. Classic Hollywood.
The Numbers Behind the Nightmare To understand why studios keep trying to make this work, check out these box office numbers:
Film Release Year Budget (Approximate) Worldwide Gross Friday the 13th (Original) 1980 $550,000 $59.8 million Friday the 13th (2009) 2009 $19 million $91.4 million Freddy vs. Jason 2003 $25 million $114.3 million The 2009 reboot made nearly five times its budget globally. In Hollywood, that kind of return usually means immediate sequels, not a decade-plus disappearing act.
What This Means for Horror Fans The Friday the 13th situation shows how business interests can completely derail creative franchises. It’s not that horror fans wouldn’t line up for a new Jason movie tomorrow—they absolutely would. It’s not that the franchise is out of ideas—it’s reinvented itself multiple times. It’s about legal rights so tangled that no one wants to risk investing millions in a movie that could get tied up in court.
While we wait for the legal mess to sort itself out, there’s a silver lining. The existing movies are easier to watch
than ever, with streaming services making the entire franchise available at the click of a button. Plus, there’s a thriving fan community keeping Jason alive through podcasts, fan films, and conventions.
The Bottom Line Friday the 13th is more than
just a horror franchise—it’s a case study in how complicated rights can stall even the most popular cultural phenomena. While we might not get a new Jason movie anytime soon, the existing films have cemented their place in horror history.
If you’re a frustrated fan, here’s what you can do:
Revisit (or discover) the existing films Join the fan community and keep the conversation going Make noise on social media about wanting a new movie Remember that even Jason Voorhees, the ultimate unstoppable killer, can be stopped by something as mundane as paperwork The next time Friday the 13th rolls around on the calendar, you’ll have plenty of movies to marathon—and now you’ll know exactly why we haven’t gotten any new ones.
summary:
Why We Haven’t Seen a New Friday the 13th Movie in 16 Years

Sixteen years.

That’s how long horror fans have been waiting for a new Friday the 13th movie. In an era where horror franchises get rebooted more often than we change our phones, the absence of Jason Voorhees from theaters feels downright suspicious. Here’s what’s really going on with our favorite hockey-mask-wearing, machete-wielding killer.
title: Jason's 16-Year Absence Explained

content:
Costco vs Walmart: Which Actually Saves You More Money? We’ve all been there - standing in the grocery aisle, calculator app open, trying to figure out if that giant bulk package at Costco is actually cheaper than the regular-sized version at Walmart.
It’s exhausting! With prices climbing 25.5% between 2020 and 2024, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, every dollar counts more than ever.
So which store actually saves you more money? Time to check what the data really says.
The Big Picture: It’s Not Just About Sticker Prices When Consumer Reports analyzed price differences across grocery stores in six U.S. cities, they found something pretty staggering: the price gap between the highest and lowest-priced stores in each city was more than 33%.
That’s huge! And most Americans are already shopping at multiple retailers each week, probably because they sense these differences.
But when it comes to Costco vs Walmart specifically, we need to look deeper than just which has the lowest price on paper towels.
What the Price Data Actually Shows Getting perfect side-by-side comparisons is tough because stores don’t exactly make it easy. But here’s what we know from recent pricing analysis:
Price tracking services like Priceviewer and Actowiz Solutions monitor millions of products across retailers DataWeave tracked over 28,225 products across 11 categories Category price variations can be dramatic - for example, one month saw Dairy and Eggs prices jump 25.2% at some retailers while other categories remained stable This tells us that prices aren’t just different between stores - they’re constantly changing, and the “winner” depends heavily on what you’re buying.
Honestly, it’s enough to make your head spin!
The Membership Equation: Does Costco Pay for Itself? Let’s do some real math on that $60 annual Costco membership:
One Reddit user pointed out that Kirkland cetirizine (the generic version of Zyrtec) costs $17.99 for 200 pills at Costco
At a regular pharmacy, you’d pay $19.99 for just 30 pills
That’s a savings of about $105.96 just on allergy medication alone
So in this case, the membership pays for itself nearly twice over with just one purchase
And that’s before we even talk about:
Costco gas (which is typically 10-20 cents per gallon cheaper) The $1.50 hot dog-and-drink combo in the food court Prescription drug discounts If you have a car, the gas savings alone probably covers your membership fee if you fill up monthly.
Seriously, the hot dog deal alone is almost worth the membership!
Category-by-Category Breakdown Based on what shoppers report and pricing data, here’s how the stores generally compare:
Where Costco Usually Wins
Bulk paper products Over-the-counter medications Spices and baking supplies Gas Alcohol Coffee Where Walmart Is Cheaper
Produce (especially when you don’t need 5 pounds of it) Smaller quantities of meat Name-brand packaged foods Non-food household items It’s a Toss-Up
Dairy Frozen foods Cleaning supplies Remember: These are generalizations. Your specific location, timing, and shopping habits can change everything.
Quality: The Hidden Factor Price isn’t everything. As one Redditor wisely put it: “I consider quality and ethics as part of my frugality. Garbage for less money that gets trashed quickly is not frugal.”
Costco has built its reputation on quality - their Kirkland Signature products often match or exceed the quality of national brands. They also have an incredibly generous return policy. If something doesn’t meet your standards, you can typically return it, even partially used.
Walmart, on the other hand, focuses on everyday low prices and convenience. Their product selection is broader, and you can shop in smaller quantities.
The Ethics Question Several readers brought up ethical considerations. Costco generally pays higher wages than Walmart and offers better benefits. While not perfect, as noted in reports about labor practices and supply chain transparency, many people feel better supporting Costco’s employment practices.
As one Redditor bluntly stated: “I don’t give a shit if Walmart is marginally cheaper on some things, fuck Walmart.” That’s harsh, but it represents a real sentiment many shoppers share.
Making the Right Choice for Your Wallet So which should you choose? Here’s a practical approach:
Stick with one store if:
You hate shopping and want efficiency You have limited storage space You live alone or have a small household You choose Walmart for everyday needs and don’t want to pay a membership fee Use both stores if:
You have storage space for bulk items You have a larger family You want to maximize savings on specific categories You can make the membership fee back in savings My recommendation: Get a Costco membership if you can make it work financially, but supplement with Walmart trips for items where:
You don’t need bulk quantities Walmart has a significantly better price Costco doesn’t carry the product Think of it this way - your membership gives you access to Costco’s best deals, while Walmart fills in the gaps for everything else.
This is basically how I shop and it works great for my budget!
The Real Deal Costco isn’t always cheaper on every single item, but when they’re cheaper, they’re often dramatically cheaper. Plus, you’re getting better quality and supporting a company with better labor practices.
The key is being strategic about where you buy what. Focus on Costco for bulk purchases of non-perishables, medications, paper products, and gas. Use Walmart for produce, small quantities, and items where you see a clear price advantage.
At the end of the day, smart shopping isn’t about choosing one store over the other forever. It’s about making informed decisions on where your money goes and getting the most value for every dollar you spend.
summary:
Costco vs Walmart: Which Actually Saves You More Money?

We’ve all been there - standing in the grocery aisle, calculator app open, trying to figure out if that giant bulk package at Costco is actually cheaper than the regular-sized version at Walmart.

It’s exhausting! With prices climbing 25.5% between 2020 and 2024, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, every dollar counts more than ever.
title: Costco vs Walmart: Which Is Actually Cheaper?
